evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to reflect actual scope — e.g. 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend) [selection infrastructure only; no consumer rewiring]' — so it does not conflict with the Scope section.
evidence: Title: 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions'
Scope: 'Out of scope: rewiring `_load_acli` / the `AcliClient` construction sites / the differ/apply leaks (all S4)'
Context: 'It deliberately does not rewire any existing consumer'
findings.finding: The plan title ('route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions') directly contradicts the story body, which explicitly places consumer rewiring out of scope and defers it to S4. An executor reading only the title would build the wrong thing.
findings.location: Plan title vs. Scope section ('Out of scope: rewiring _load_acli / the AcliClient construction sites')
scenarios: An executor reads the title, skips the scope section, and spends time modifying _load_acli and the AcliClient construction sites — exactly the trap the first draft hit.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend) — selection infrastructure only (S3)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen import-forcing mechanism explicitly (e.g. 'select_backend directly imports adapters/jira/backend.py before resolving; future adapters are added similarly') rather than leaving it as an 'or' for the executor.
evidence: 'select_backend imports that module (or a small adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving'
The choice between a direct per-module import inside select_backend vs. a centralized __init__ aggregator has different extensibility and coupling implications.
findings.finding: The import-forcing mechanism for adapter registration is left as an unresolved 'or' choice for the executor: 'select_backend imports that module (or a small adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter)'. This is a real design decision (a single explicit import vs. an __init__ aggregator) that affects the public API surface and how future adapters are registered.
findings.location: Approach section, sentence: 'Registration happens at import of adapters/jira/backend.py; select_backend imports that module (or a small adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter)'
scenarios: Executor picks the __init__ aggregator approach; a later reviewer expected direct-import; the two conflict when S4 adds a second adapter and the pattern is already set.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one: 'select_backend directly imports `adapters.jira.backend` (and each additional shipped adapter) before resolving — no __init__ aggregator.' This keeps the coupling explicit and avoids a hidden indirection until a second backend exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the story title to reflect only the selection infrastructure delivered here (e.g. 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend)') and remove 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions' from the title to eliminate the contradiction with Scope/Context.
evidence: Title: 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions'
Context: 'It deliberately does not rewire any existing consumer'
Scope Out of scope: 'rewiring _load_acli / the AcliClient construction sites / the differ/apply leaks (all S4)'
findings.finding: The story title names 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions' as a deliverable of this story, directly contradicting the Scope and Context sections, which explicitly defer that rewiring to S4.
findings.location: Story title vs. Scope / Context sections
scenarios: An implementer reading only the title could begin rewiring AcliClient construction sites, then discover the Scope contradicts this — wasting time or producing an out-of-scope diff.
findings.suggested_fix: Title: 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend)' (drop the routing clause entirely, since that is S4's responsibility).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the triggering-import mechanism (either an `adapters/__init__.py` shim or an explicit import inside `select_backend`) to the Edit-set as a named deliverable, and add an AC or test note verifying that the registry is populated when `select_backend` is called without any prior manual import of the adapter.
evidence: Approach: 'Registration happens at import of adapters/jira/backend.py; select_backend imports that module (or a small adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving, so the registry is populated deterministically regardless of import order.'
Edit-set lists only: config.py, _config_schema.py, _backend_registry.py, adapters/jira/backend.py
No AC references the triggering-import mechanism.
findings.finding: The Approach states that `select_backend` imports `adapters/jira/backend.py` (or a small `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter) to ensure the registry is populated deterministically, but the Edit-set does not list any triggering-import file (e.g. `adapters/__init__.py`) as a file to create/modify, and no AC verifies this mechanism exists.
findings.location: Approach section ('Registration happens at import … select_backend imports that module or a small adapters/__init__') vs. Edit-set (four files listed, no __init__ or import shim)
scenarios: Implementer adds the `@register` decorator to JiraBackend but forgets to add the eagerly-triggered import inside `select_backend`; the registry is empty at call time and `select_backend` raises a config error for `"jira"` even with the default config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the Teeth scenario: `select_backend` dispatches to a non-default registered backend when config names that key, verified by the FakeBackend test using `_reset_registry_for_test`.
evidence: Testing: 'Teeth: register a FakeBackend under a test key via the reset helper, point a config at it, and assert select_backend returns the fake — confirming selection is honored — then reset.'
AC #2 covers JiraBackend equivalence only; AC #3 covers register idempotency and reset round-trip, but not the full select-under-fake-key scenario.
findings.finding: The 'Teeth' test (register a FakeBackend under a test key, call select_backend, assert the fake is returned, then reset) is described in the Testing section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion — it validates a key behavioral guarantee (that selection is honored end-to-end) that AC #2 and AC #3 do not individually cover.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC #2, #3)
scenarios: The Teeth test is removed during review as 'not required by any AC', weakening confidence that the registry dispatch path works for non-default keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section (or prominent module-level docstring in `_backend_registry.py`) documenting the three-step 'add a backend' protocol: (1) add key to static frozenset in `_config_schema.py`, (2) create `adapters/<x>/backend.py` with `@register("<x>", factory)`, (3) import it in `select_backend`'s bootstrap path — and explain why both the static set and the registry exist.
evidence: 'the *static* set guards config-load; the *registry* does the authoritative runtime lookup' — two sources of truth for valid backend keys
'a second backend is added in-tree under `adapters/<x>/`' — implies future contributors must update frozenset + create adapter + add to import bootstrap, but no doc captures this
No ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan

'Registration happens at import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`; `select_backend` imports that module (or a small `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving' — the bootstrap import path is described but not documented as a convention
findings.finding: The plan introduces a two-layer validation contract (static frozenset in `_config_schema.py` + runtime registry in `_backend_registry.py` + import-bootstrap in `select_backend`) that is novel and non-obvious for future contributors adding a backend. No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or in-code convention document is planned to capture this multi-step protocol, making it a silent maintainability trap.
scenarios: A developer adds a second backend (`adapters/foo/backend.py`, `@register("foo", ...)`) but forgets to update the static frozenset in `_config_schema.py` — config with `reconciler.backend = "foo"` is rejected at load time with a confusing 'not in allowed keys' error even though the registry has it
A developer updates the frozenset but forgets to add the import to the bootstrap path — config passes schema validation but `select_backend` raises 'unknown key' at runtime
No one knows which of the two mechanisms is authoritative, leading to divergence over time
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `_backend_registry.py` like: 'HOW TO ADD A BACKEND: (1) Add the key to `KNOWN_BACKEND_KEYS` in `_config_schema.py`. (2) Create `adapters/<key>/backend.py`, decorate your `Backend` subclass with `@register("<key>", YourBackend)`. (3) Import `adapters.<key>.backend` in `_ensure_adapters_loaded()` inside this module. The static set provides fast config-load rejection with a clear error; the registry provides the authoritative runtime factory lookup.' Also reference this from the AGENTS.md or a brief ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the thread-safety contract of the registry: either (a) document that `register` / `_reset_registry_for_test` are only safe at import time or under a single-threaded test runner, or (b) protect the module-level dict with a `threading.Lock` around the check-then-act in `register` and `_reset_registry_for_test`.
evidence: 'The registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent register decorator'
'_reset_registry_for_test restores the prior state'
'register a FakeBackend under a test key via the reset helper, point a config at it' — dynamic registration at test-runtime, not just at import time
No mention of a lock, threading.Lock, or thread-safety scope anywhere in the plan
Idempotency spec ('same key+factory = no-op; conflicting factory raises') requires a compound check-then-act on the dict
findings.finding: The plan specifies idempotency semantics for `register` (same key+factory = no-op; conflicting factory raises) but says nothing about concurrency safety of the check-then-act pattern on the module-level registry dict. At module-import time, Python's import lock serializes registrations, so production use is safe — but the plan also requires tests to call `_reset_registry_for_test` and then register a FakeBackend dynamically. If tests run in parallel threads (e.g., pytest-xdist with threading mode, or future async test infrastructure), two test threads could both read 'key absent', both pass the guard, and both write — one silently clobbering the other or producing a false conflict error. The plan has no lock, no mention of thread-safety scope, and no guidance on whether the registry is safe for dynamic (non-import-time) registration.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent `register` decorator'; Testing section — 'register a `FakeBackend` under a test key via the reset helper'
scenarios: pytest-xdist or any parallel-threaded test runner: two test threads both call `_reset_registry_for_test` + `register('fake', FakeBackend)` concurrently; the check-then-act on the dict is not atomic, so one thread may see a stale empty registry while another has already written, either silently losing a registration or spuriously raising the 'conflicting factory' error, causing a flaky test failure that does not reproduce serially.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `threading.Lock` (e.g., `_registry_lock = threading.Lock()`) to `_backend_registry.py` and acquire it around the compound check-then-write in `register` and around the swap in `_reset_registry_for_test`. Document in the module docstring that dynamic registration (outside import time) is thread-safe under this lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the story to reflect what it actually delivers (e.g. 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend) — selection infrastructure only') so the title matches the explicitly deferred scope.
evidence: Title: 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions'
Body Scope: 'Out of scope: rewiring _load_acli / the AcliClient construction sites / the differ/apply leaks (all S4)'
Context/Problem: 'It deliberately does not rewire any existing consumer'
findings.finding: The title promises 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions' but the body explicitly defers that routing to S4, meaning an agent or engineer reading only the title would attempt to rewire AcliClient call-sites that the plan forbids touching.
scenarios: An implementer reading the ticket title scopes their work to include AcliClient rewiring, conflicts with the explicit out-of-scope constraint, and either over-delivers (breaking S4's clean boundary) or is confused about what 'done' means at PR review.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to something like: 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend) [selection infrastructure; AcliClient rewire deferred to S4]'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `reconciler.backend` validation should live in the existing top-level `rebar._config_schema._SECTIONS['reconciler']` dict (using the already-present `_as_choice` helper) rather than a new reconciler-local `_config_schema.py`, to avoid creating a parallel validation infrastructure.
evidence: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:117`: `def _as_choice(v: Any, key: str, choices: set[str]) -> str:` — already exists and raises `ConfigError` naming allowed keys.

`src/rebar/_config_schema.py:605-613`: `_as_choice` is already used for `sync.push/{always,async,off}`, `sync.pull/{on,off}`, `mcp.transport/{stdio,http}` — three prior uses of this exact static-set pattern.

`src/rebar/_config_schema.py:472-489`: `ReconcilerConfig` dataclass and `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:649-655`: `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` dict — the right place to add `backend` with `_as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})`.

Plan quote: 'stdlib-only / zero-rebar-dep (it cannot import the registry), so the static set guards config-load' — this constraint is already satisfied by `_as_choice` in the existing top-level `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new reconciler-local `_config_schema.py` with a 'static frozenset of known backend keys' for config-load validation, but the codebase already has a top-level `rebar._config_schema` with `ReconcilerConfig`, `_SECTIONS['reconciler']`, and `_as_choice()` (used at lines 605, 606, 613 for `sync.push`, `sync.pull`, `mcp.transport`). Adding a second, parallel reconciler-local schema module duplicates validation infrastructure that the established pattern (`_as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` in the existing `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` dict) already handles in one line.
findings.location: Edit-set: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py` — 'validate the key against a static frozenset of known backend keys'
scenarios: Two separate schema modules both claim authority over `reconciler.*` keys, creating ambiguity about which is the source of truth for future key additions.
A contributor adding a future `reconciler.*` key must decide which schema file to edit, and may pick the wrong one.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, add `backend` to `ReconcilerConfig` (default `"jira"`) and to `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` as `"backend": lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})`. Remove the proposed new reconciler-local `_config_schema.py` file from the edit-set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unsupported 'in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere' claim with a citation of the actual closest analogue (e.g., `_as_choice`-gated static dispatch, or `typed_dispatch._build_leaves`-style static dict) or explicitly acknowledge this is the first class-factory registry in the package.
evidence: Search for `@register`, `decorator.*registry`, and `register(key, factory)` in `src/rebar/` returns zero hits matching a class-factory decorator registry.

`src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:355`, `src/rebar/metrics/sidecar_metrics.py:277`, `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:264`: `def register()` — these are zero-arg list-appenders for metric specs, structurally different from a `register(key, factory)` decorator.
Plan quote: 'the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere (no setuptools entry-points)' — this claim is not supported by the grep results.
findings.finding: The plan justifies the decorator-registry pattern by claiming it mirrors 'the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere,' but a codebase search finds no matching decorator-based class-factory registry in `src/rebar/` — the `metrics/register()` functions are plain list-appenders for metric specs, not class-factory registries. The stated prior-art does not exist.
findings.location: Approach section: 'an in-tree registry, [...] the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere — no setuptools entry-points'
scenarios: During implementation the author finds no existing pattern to mirror and must design the decorator API from scratch, potentially producing an API inconsistent with the codebase's actual idioms.
A reviewer cannot locate the cited 'existing pattern,' reducing confidence in the design rationale and making the choice harder to evaluate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere' reference with a concrete pointer to the specific existing pattern (e.g. rebar.llm.workflow.executor.register_step or rebar.attest.registry.register_scheme) and call out the difference in idempotency semantics, or provide a brief inline spec of the dict-keyed module-state pattern directly.
evidence: Plan text: 'a register(key, factory) decorator, a select_backend(config) resolver ... the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere'
Grep of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler for 'register' returns no decorator-registry pattern within the reconciler package.
Closest analogues are rebar.llm.workflow.executor.register_step and rebar.llm.criteria.overlay.register_gate — both in rebar.llm.*, not in rebar_reconciler.

rebar.llm.criteria.overlay.register_gate is explicitly idempotent-by-overwrite (re-registering overwrites), which differs from the idempotent-same/raise-on-conflict semantics the plan specifies for register.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere' as the pattern to follow for the new _backend_registry.py, implying an established in-tree analogue within rebar_reconciler itself. A grep of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler shows no decorator-registry pattern exists there; the comparable patterns (register_step, register_gate, register_scheme) live in rebar.llm.* and rebar.attest.*, which are architecturally distant subsystems with different constraints (e.g. register_gate is not idempotent by key+factory, it overwrites). The plan gives no concrete reference to the specific pattern to emulate, leaving the implementer to infer from a distant analogy that may not fit the reconciler's module-loading model.
findings.location: Approach section — 'the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere'
scenarios: Implementer picks register_gate as the template but gets overwrite-on-conflict semantics instead of the raise-on-conflict the AC and test spec require.
Implementer spends time searching for a non-existent reconciler-local pattern instead of designing from scratch.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere' with: 'A module-level dict `_REGISTRY: dict[str, Callable[[], Backend]] = {}` populated by `register(key, factory)`: if `key` already maps to the same `factory` object it is a no-op; if it maps to a different factory it raises `ValueError`. This mirrors the pattern in `rebar.attest.registry.register_scheme` but is simpler (no scheme-object wrapping). Note: `rebar.llm.criteria.overlay.register_gate` uses overwrite-on-re-register semantics and should NOT be used as a template here.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan title to reflect actual S3 scope — e.g. 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend); does NOT rewire the 5 AcliClient construction sites (S4)' — so the title matches the body's explicit out-of-scope statement.
evidence: Title: 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions'
Scope section: 'Out of scope: rewiring _load_acli / the AcliClient construction sites / the differ/apply leaks (all S4)'
AC line 4: 'No existing consumer (_load_acli, the AcliClient construction sites, the differ/apply modules) is modified'
Grep confirms all 5 AcliClient construction sites remain unmodified in current HEAD.
findings.finding: The plan title 'route the 5 inline AcliClient constructions' directly contradicts the body's explicit scope exclusion ('No consumer is rewired here — select_backend is proven equivalent to today's construction in isolation; S4 changes the call sites'). The title is an empirically-checkable assertion that this story routes the construction sites, but the body (and the scoped AC) confirm no routing happens in S3. This mismatch means a reader/reviewer scanning only the title will form a false expectation of what this story delivers.
findings.location: Plan title vs Scope section ('No consumer is rewired here')
scenarios: A reviewer or future story author reads the title and believes S3 already wired the consumer sites, causing them to skip the S4 prerequisite work.
The AC git-diff check passes (no consumer files touched) but a title-driven expectation causes confusion in PR review.
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Backend registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend) [selection infrastructure only; consumer rewire is S4]'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the edit-set: the `reconciler.backend` field and its coercer belong in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (`ReconcilerConfig` dataclass + `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` dict using the existing `_as_choice` helper), not in a new `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`.
evidence: No `_config_schema.py` found under `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` via directory listing and search.
`ReconcilerConfig` dataclass is at `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:472`; `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercers at `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:649-655`.
The existing `_as_choice` helper at `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:117` is already used for `sync.push`, `sync.pull`, `mcp.transport` — identical pattern to what the plan describes.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py` as an edit target, but this file does not exist. The reconciler's typed config schema (`ReconcilerConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer table) lives in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. Creating a new reconciler-local `_config_schema.py` would duplicate schema infrastructure and bypass the existing config-load path.
scenarios: If a new `_config_schema.py` is created inside the reconciler package, the `reconciler.backend` key would never be parsed by `rebar.config.load_config()` because that loader reads `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, not the reconciler-local one — the config-load rejection test would silently never trigger.

The reconciler-local `config.py` (the plan's other edit target) holds only runtime constants, not the typed schema; adding a `backend` key there would not wire into the config-load validation path at all.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`: (1) add `backend: str = "jira"` to `ReconcilerConfig`; (2) add `"backend": lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` to `_SECTIONS["reconciler"]`. Remove `_config_schema.py` from the reconciler-package edit-set. The reconciler-local `config.py` (constants only) does not need a `backend` key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a parity test (or generation strategy) that asserts the static frozenset in `_config_schema.py` equals the set of keys registered in `_backend_registry` at test-time, so the two layers cannot silently diverge when a new backend is added.
evidence: Plan: 'validate the key against a **static frozenset** of known backend keys (e.g. `{"jira"}`). The schema is stdlib-only / zero-rebar-dep (it cannot import the registry), so the *static* set guards config-load; the *registry* does the authoritative runtime lookup.'
No test or generation step is mentioned that asserts `_config_schema._KNOWN_BACKEND_KEYS == set(_backend_registry._REGISTRY.keys())`.
The S2 ticket (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) confirms a second backend is anticipated ('a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/'), making this a near-term real-world scenario.
findings.finding: The plan creates two independent sources of truth for valid backend keys — a static `frozenset` in `_config_schema.py` and the live registry in `_backend_registry.py` — with no specified mechanism to keep them in sync. When a second backend is added to the registry but the frozenset is not updated (or vice versa), users receive contradictory errors depending on which layer runs first (config-load rejects a key the registry accepts, or config-load accepts a key the registry misses at runtime). The plan's positive framing that 'the registry does the authoritative runtime lookup' is undermined if the static guard can contradict it, but no parity test or generation strategy is specified.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a static frozenset of known backend keys… the registry does the authoritative runtime lookup'; _config_schema.py + _backend_registry.py edit-set
scenarios: A developer adds `adapters/linear/backend.py` with `@register('linear', LinearBackendFactory)` but forgets to add `'linear'` to the schema frozenset — config-load rejects the key with 'not in allowed set' before select_backend ever runs, despite the registry accepting it.

A developer adds `'linear'` to the frozenset before the adapter is written — config-load accepts the key, but select_backend raises 'unknown backend' at runtime, a confusing error whose message lists registered keys (which don't include 'linear' yet).
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, add a unit test: `assert _config_schema.KNOWN_BACKEND_KEYS == set(_backend_registry._registry_keys())`. Alternatively, have `_config_schema.py` import the frozenset from a thin, stdlib-safe constant module that `_backend_registry.py` also references, making them a single source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `backend: str = "jira"` to `ReconcilerConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` and a matching static-key coercer to `_SECTIONS["reconciler"]`, and include `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` in the plan's edit-set (the reconciler-local `_config_schema.py` may supplement it but cannot replace it for the live `load_config()` path).
evidence: Plan names a NEW `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py` described as 'stdlib-only / zero-rebar-dep (it cannot import the registry)'
Real config pipeline: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` lines 472-488 define `ReconcilerConfig`; lines 649-655 define `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercers — neither mentions `backend`
`select_backend(config)` is described as reading `config.reconciler.backend` — this attribute does not exist unless `ReconcilerConfig` is extended
AC: 'a value outside the static known-key set is rejected at config-load' — only achievable if the root `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` coercer is updated
ABSENCE: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (root) is absent from the plan's edit-set
findings.finding: The plan's edit-set omits `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (the root schema), which is the only file that `coerce_sparse` / `Config.from_mapping` actually reads at `load_config()` time. `ReconcilerConfig` (line 472) and `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` (line 649) must gain a `backend` field and coercer respectively, or `config.reconciler.backend` will not exist on the object `select_backend(config)` receives, causing an `AttributeError` at runtime and leaving the 'rejected at config-load' AC hollow.
scenarios: `select_backend(load_config())` raises `AttributeError: 'ReconcilerConfig' object has no attribute 'backend'` in production
Config-load unit test using `_config_schema.py`'s static validator passes in isolation but `make test` fails on any test that exercises the real `load_config()` → `select_backend()` path

An operator sets `reconciler.backend = 'nope'` in `rebar.toml`; the root coercer table has no handler for the key, so it is warned-as-unknown (or silently dropped) rather than rejected with a helpful error
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`: (1) add `backend: str = "jira"` to `ReconcilerConfig`; (2) add `"backend": lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` to `_SECTIONS["reconciler"]`. The reconciler-local `_config_schema.py` can then be dropped entirely (the root schema IS the static validator), or repurposed as a thin re-export so the reconciler package remains importable without pulling in the root schema.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `adapters/jira/backend.py` is decorated with `@register('jira', ...)` and that registration happens at import time, but does not specify which module triggers the import of `adapters/jira/backend.py` before `select_backend(config)` is first called. The existing call-site modules (`acli.py`, `applier.py`, `fetcher.py`, `run_differs.py`, `_attestation.py`) currently have no import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`, so if `_backend_registry.py` itself does not explicitly import it, the registry will be empty at resolution time.
findings.location: Edit-set: 'adapters/jira/backend.py — decorate JiraBackend with @register("jira", …)'; Approach: 'import-time registration'
findings.suggested_fix: Have `_backend_registry.py` include a `_load_builtin_backends()` function (called at module bottom or lazily in `select_backend`) that imports `adapters.jira.backend`; this keeps the registry self-contained and mirrors how `typed_dispatch._build_leaves()` populates itself eagerly at module import time.

findings.finding: The `_load_acli()` seam in both `applier.py` and `fetcher.py` currently returns the `acli` MODULE (callers do `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)`); the plan says the functions will 'delegate to `select_backend`' while existing tests 'still pass unchanged'. These goals are mechanically incompatible: `select_backend()` returns a `Backend` object, not a module with an `.AcliClient` attribute, so tests patching `_load_acli` to return a fake module with `.AcliClient` would break unless the delegation wrapper reconstructs the module-shaped interface — which the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Approach section: 'keep the functions, have them delegate to select_backend'; AC line 4: 'existing tests that patch them still pass unchanged'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `config.reconciler.backend` is validated against the registry's known keys at config-load time, but the registry is only populated when `adapters/jira/backend.py` is imported (import-time registration). If config validation is triggered before that import has occurred — e.g., during a cold startup where config is parsed before the jira adapter module is on the import path — the registry will be empty and the default value `"jira"` will fail its own validation. The plan does not specify who is responsible for ensuring the jira adapter is imported before any config validation runs, nor does it define an import ordering contract.
findings.location: Context / Problem — `_backend_registry.py` design; AC line: "`config.reconciler.backend` exists, defaults to `"jira"`, and rejects an unregistered value with an error listing the registered keys`"
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend_registry.py`, add a deferred-validation mode: rather than checking against registered keys at config-parse time, validate at `select_backend()` call time (when the registry is guaranteed populated). Alternatively, have `_backend_registry.py` itself import `adapters/jira/backend` unconditionally at the bottom of the file to guarantee registration before any consumer can call `select_backend` or validate config.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
